stage.name: CV32A60X vs CV32A60AX: same family, different conclusions
stage.description: A source-grounded comparison lesson that teaches learners to compare CVA6 variants before making privilege, MMU, cache, or application-profile claims.
stage.courseTemplateName: Professional Technical Deep Dive - Evidence-Grounded SlideKit

content.title: CV32A60X vs CV32A60AX: same family, different conclusions
content.subtitle: Two 32-bit CVA6 configurations can share family DNA while changing the safe answer to memory, privilege, and cache questions.
content.thesis: Do not compare names. Compare evidence rows.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 User Manual configuration table
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The User Manual identifies CV32A60AX, CV32A60X, and CV64A6_MMU as verified configurations with different short descriptions, privilege levels, ISA strings, and CV-X-IF support.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 Requirements initial release table
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The initial release table compares CV32A60X and CV32A60AX by target, ISA, XLEN, FPU, CV-X-IF, MMU, and L1 instruction/data cache size.

content.speakerNotes: This lesson is a reality check. CV32A60X and CV32A60AX look close enough that a learner might treat them as basically the same thing. The documents do not support that shortcut. When a question involves privilege modes, MMU, cache size, or application profile, those small letters in the name can lead to a different engineering conclusion.

actions.text: This lesson is a reality check. CV32A60X and CV32A60AX look close enough that a learner might treat them as basically the same thing. The documents do not support that shortcut. When a question involves privilege modes, MMU, cache size, or application profile, those small letters in the name can lead to a different engineering conclusion.

scenes.title: Start with the official comparison rows

content.title: The table tells you what can and cannot travel between variants
content.subtitle: Read the comparison table before borrowing a claim from one configuration to another.

cards.title: CV32A60X
cards.body: A 32-bit embedded core profile, machine-only in the user manual table, with no MMU in the requirements release table.

cards.title: CV32A60AX
cards.body: A 32-bit application core profile with machine, supervisor, and user levels, and Sv32 MMU in the release table.

cards.title: Shared signals
cards.body: Both are ASIC targets, 32-bit, IMC in the requirements table, no FPU there, and CV-X-IF is listed as supported.

cards.title: Different conclusions
cards.body: Privilege, virtual memory, and cache-related statements must not be copied blindly across the rows.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 User Manual configuration table
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The User Manual identifies CV32A60AX, CV32A60X, and CV64A6_MMU as verified configurations with different short descriptions, privilege levels, ISA strings, and CV-X-IF support.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 Requirements initial release table
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The initial release table compares CV32A60X and CV32A60AX by target, ISA, XLEN, FPU, CV-X-IF, MMU, and L1 instruction/data cache size.

content.speakerNotes: The official rows are the first stop. CV32A60X is the embedded, machine-only path in the user manual table, and the requirements table lists no MMU. CV32A60AX is the application profile with machine, supervisor, and user levels, and Sv32 in the MMU column. They share some facts, but the differences are exactly the facts that matter in architecture teaching.

actions.text: The official rows are the first stop. CV32A60X is the embedded, machine-only path in the user manual table, and the requirements table lists no MMU. CV32A60AX is the application profile with machine, supervisor, and user levels, and Sv32 in the MMU column. They share some facts, but the differences are exactly the facts that matter in architecture teaching.

content.title: Embedded profile and application profile are not interchangeable
content.subtitle: The same family can support different teaching paths depending on the selected configuration.

columns.title: CV32A60X path
columns.body: Use it as the first stable embedded-style target: machine-only, no MMU in the release table, smaller L1 cache numbers, and a concrete design document.

columns.title: CV32A60AX path
columns.body: Use it when the lesson needs application-core concerns such as supervisor/user privilege and Sv32 virtual memory.

content.takeaway: A variant comparison is not trivia; it decides what the next architecture lesson is allowed to teach.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 User Manual configuration table
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The User Manual identifies CV32A60AX, CV32A60X, and CV64A6_MMU as verified configurations with different short descriptions, privilege levels, ISA strings, and CV-X-IF support.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 Requirements initial release table
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The initial release table compares CV32A60X and CV32A60AX by target, ISA, XLEN, FPU, CV-X-IF, MMU, and L1 instruction/data cache size.

content.speakerNotes: This is the core distinction. CV32A60X is a better first target for a compact, source-grounded embedded profile. CV32A60AX is where application-core ideas like supervisor mode, user mode, and Sv32 become relevant. If a lesson on virtual memory silently uses CV32A60X evidence, it will confuse learners. The selected variant decides the permitted storyline.

actions.text: This is the core distinction. CV32A60X is a better first target for a compact, source-grounded embedded profile. CV32A60AX is where application-core ideas like supervisor mode, user mode, and Sv32 become relevant. If a lesson on virtual memory silently uses CV32A60X evidence, it will confuse learners. The selected variant decides the permitted storyline.

content.title: Turn each statement into a variant-scoped claim
content.subtitle: This is the habit that keeps published lessons professional.

items.title: Width claim
items.body: Safe: both rows are 32-bit in the release table. Still name the table you used.

items.title: MMU claim
items.body: Danger: CV32A60X and CV32A60AX differ, so never generalize from one row.

items.title: Privilege claim
items.body: Danger: machine-only versus machine/supervisor/user changes software and trap discussions.

items.title: Cache claim
items.body: Danger: the release table lists different L1 cache sizes, so performance wording must be scoped.

items.title: CV-X-IF claim
items.body: Safer: both rows list support, but still cite the configuration evidence.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 Requirements initial release table
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The initial release table compares CV32A60X and CV32A60AX by target, ISA, XLEN, FPU, CV-X-IF, MMU, and L1 instruction/data cache size.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X parameter configuration table
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CV32A60X parameter table records concrete values such as XLEN 32, VLEN 32, RVC true, MmuPresent false, RVS false, RVU false, CvxifEn true, PipelineOnly true, and single commit port behavior.

content.speakerNotes: A professional lesson filters every sentence. A 32-bit statement is relatively safe across these two rows, but cite the row. An MMU statement is not safe, because one row says none and the other says Sv32. Privilege is not safe either. Cache-size language is also scoped. Even CV-X-IF, which both rows support, should still point to configuration evidence.

actions.text: A professional lesson filters every sentence. A 32-bit statement is relatively safe across these two rows, but cite the row. An MMU statement is not safe, because one row says none and the other says Sv32. Privilege is not safe either. Cache-size language is also scoped. Even CV-X-IF, which both rows support, should still point to configuration evidence.

content.title: Compare by conclusion type
content.subtitle: Do not compare every parameter at once. Compare the ones that affect the question you are answering.

steps.title: Name the question
steps.body: Is it about ISA, privilege, memory translation, cache behavior, interface support, or pipeline shape?

steps.title: Select rows
steps.body: Choose the exact configurations you are comparing.

steps.title: Extract changing fields
steps.body: Pull only fields that can change the answer.

steps.title: Write scoped result
steps.body: Say what is true for each variant and what cannot be generalized.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 User Manual configuration table
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The User Manual identifies CV32A60AX, CV32A60X, and CV64A6_MMU as verified configurations with different short descriptions, privilege levels, ISA strings, and CV-X-IF support.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 Parameters and Configuration catalog
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The Parameters and Configuration page lists CVA6 parameters such as XLEN, VLEN, MmuPresent, RVS, RVU, CvxifEn, cache sizes, SuperscalarEn, commit ports, and PipelineOnly.

content.speakerNotes: The reusable method is simple. First name the question. If the question is about privilege, do not stare at cache rows. If it is about integration, inspect interface fields. Then choose the exact configuration rows, extract only the fields that change the answer, and write the result with scope. This keeps the lesson short but technically strong.

actions.text: The reusable method is simple. First name the question. If the question is about privilege, do not stare at cache rows. If it is about integration, inspect interface fields. Then choose the exact configuration rows, extract only the fields that change the answer, and write the result with scope. This keeps the lesson short but technically strong.

scenes.title: Why this course starts with CV32A60X

content.title: Use one baseline before branching
content.subtitle: The series can cover more variants later, but the first architecture path needs a stable baseline.

columns.title: Baseline benefit
columns.body: CV32A60X gives us a concrete design document and a parameter table, so early lessons can stay source-grounded.

columns.title: Branching later
columns.body: Once learners know the baseline, later lessons can introduce AX, MMU, or wider CV64A6 topics as explicit branches.

content.takeaway: A focused baseline is not a limitation. It is how learners avoid mixing incompatible facts.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design document objective
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CV32A60X design document describes the CV32A60X configuration version of CVA6 and is intended as formal configuration-specific design documentation.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X parameter configuration table
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CV32A60X parameter table records concrete values such as XLEN 32, VLEN 32, RVC true, MmuPresent false, RVS false, RVU false, CvxifEn true, PipelineOnly true, and single commit port behavior.

content.speakerNotes: This also explains our course design. We start with CV32A60X because it gives us a concrete design document and stable parameter evidence. That does not mean AX or CV64A6_MMU are unimportant. It means we first build one clean mental model, then branch deliberately. Learners remember better when the baseline is sharp instead of watery.

actions.text: This also explains our course design. We start with CV32A60X because it gives us a concrete design document and stable parameter evidence. That does not mean AX or CV64A6_MMU are unimportant. It means we first build one clean mental model, then branch deliberately. Learners remember better when the baseline is sharp instead of watery.

questions.question: Which conclusion must not be copied from CV32A60AX to CV32A60X without checking evidence?

options.label: The fact that both are CVA6-family configurations
That both are 32-bit rows in the release table
That CV-X-IF appears in the comparison rows

questions.analysis: The user manual table distinguishes CV32A60AX as machine/supervisor/user while CV32A60X is machine-only.

questions.question: What does the requirements initial release table list for CV32A60X MMU support?

options.label: Unspecified and always inferred from AX

questions.analysis: The release table lists CV32A60X with MMU set to None, while CV32A60AX is listed with Sv32.

questions.question: Which fields can change the safe explanation between CV32A60X and CV32A60AX?
questions.analysis: Privilege, MMU, and cache size affect architecture and software explanations. The shared family name does not make all conclusions portable.

questions.question: Why is CV32A60X still a good early baseline for this course?

options.label: It has a concrete design document and parameter table that support source-grounded lessons
It proves every CVA6 variant has the same MMU behavior
It removes the need to discuss configuration
It is always more advanced than AX

questions.analysis: A strong baseline is chosen for teaching traceability, not because it represents every possible CVA6 configuration.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 User Manual configuration table
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The User Manual identifies CV32A60AX, CV32A60X, and CV64A6_MMU as verified configurations with different short descriptions, privilege levels, ISA strings, and CV-X-IF support.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 Requirements initial release table
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The initial release table compares CV32A60X and CV32A60AX by target, ISA, XLEN, FPU, CV-X-IF, MMU, and L1 instruction/data cache size.

content.speakerNotes: The checkpoint is designed to catch the most common learner mistake: treating similar variant names as interchangeable facts. CV32A60AX privilege support does not automatically move to CV32A60X. The MMU column differs. Cache numbers differ. But CV32A60X remains an excellent baseline because it gives us concrete, configuration-specific source evidence for early architecture lessons.

actions.text: The checkpoint is designed to catch the most common learner mistake: treating similar variant names as interchangeable facts. CV32A60AX privilege support does not automatically move to CV32A60X. The MMU column differs. Cache numbers differ. But CV32A60X remains an excellent baseline because it gives us concrete, configuration-specific source evidence for early architecture lessons.

scenes.title: Variant comparison is now a skill

content.title: You can now prevent variant drift
content.subtitle: Variant drift happens when a lesson quietly imports facts from the wrong configuration.
takeaways: CV32A60X and CV32A60AX share a family context but differ in privilege, MMU, and cache-related evidence.
A safe lesson compares the fields that affect the technical question being answered.
The CVA6 From Zero path starts with CV32A60X as a baseline, then branches only when the evidence supports it.
content.nextStep: Close Unit 02 with a checkpoint that tests family, profile, parameters, workflow, and variant-scoped claims.
content.closing: The badge on the row is not enough. The fields in the row are where the engineering truth lives.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 User Manual configuration table
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The User Manual identifies CV32A60AX, CV32A60X, and CV64A6_MMU as verified configurations with different short descriptions, privilege levels, ISA strings, and CV-X-IF support.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 Requirements initial release table
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The initial release table compares CV32A60X and CV32A60AX by target, ISA, XLEN, FPU, CV-X-IF, MMU, and L1 instruction/data cache size.

content.speakerNotes: You now have a variant-comparison habit. CV32A60X and CV32A60AX are close in name, but the fields that matter are not identical. The next lesson closes Unit 02 with a checkpoint. If you can pass it, you are ready to enter the architecture spine without dragging the wrong configuration assumptions along for the ride.
